items.title: SCI-FI is all you need: Meet GEN-I-SYS
items.author: Ashish Waikar
items.content:
I'm a huge fan of the Marvel Iron Man series, especially the tech aspects that make so much of what we see feel achievable today and in the near future. Technologies like J.A.R.V.I.S seem much closer to reality than we could have imagined just 5-6 years ago.

I wanted to learn about it in an interesting and fun way, so I decided to work on something cool that would challenge me.

The timing couldn't have been better: there's a Codédex community monthly event featuring a "build a bot" challenge, which provided perfect motivation for the project. I'd also been reading about libraries like browseruse that I was eager to explore. This felt like the ideal opportunity for some hands-on experimentation.

Introducing new fun project: GEN-I-SYS

Here's the exciting project I'm currently developing: , an AI assistant that can talk, conduct research, and code. It harnesses Google Search to find exactly what you need. GEN-I-SYS can literally open and control a web browser for you (filling out forms, navigating websites, and handling complex web tasks) while you watch it work in real-time.

    Real-time Voice Interaction: Enjoy hands-free communication with the AI through real-time audio streaming for responses. You can even interrupt it mid-conversation!

    : A sleek, modern navigation hub that provides seamless access to all integrated tools.

    : Features beautiful 3D hourglass animations to keep your productivity sessions engaging.

    Task List & Activity Matrix: Track your productivity with a GitHub-style activity heatmap that visualizes your work patterns.

    : Leverages bi-directional Server-Sent Events (SSE) to stream both text and audio data from the backend, creating a fluid, natural conversational experience.

    : GENISYS can write code into a built-in code editor that allows you to write, edit, and run HTML, CSS, and JavaScript code directly in the browser with syntax highlighting and real-time preview.

    Advanced Browsing Capabilities: Can autonomously open browsers and perform complex web actions—it's like having a digital assistant that actually gets things done! ✨

    : Personalize GEN-I-SYS with various voices and personality traits to make it truly yours.

    GithubMeet GENISYS (sharing all the updates about the project here)

    I'm constantly iterating and adding new capabilities. Some ideas brewing for future versions include integration with more specialized tools, more AI Agents, and maybe even some AR visualization features (because why not dream big?).

    I'd love to hear your thoughts on this project! Drop a comment below or reach out, I'm always excited about the wild possibilities we're building toward.

    Also, if you're participating in the Codédex build-a-bot challenge or working on similar AI projects, let's connect! The community aspect of learning and building together makes this journey even more rewarding.

    Who knows? Maybe we're all just a few iterations away from having our own J.A.R.V.I.S. Until then, the adventure continues!

items.title: Machine Learning Fundamentals: adam optimizer tutorial
items.author: DevOps Fundamental
items.content:
Adam Optimizer Tutorial: A Production Systems Perspective

Last quarter, a critical anomaly in our fraud detection system resulted in a 12% increase in false positives, triggering a cascade of customer support tickets and a temporary halt to new account creation. Root cause analysis revealed a subtle drift in model weights during a seemingly routine retraining cycle. The issue wasn’t the data, nor the model architecture, but an unmonitored interaction between the Adam optimizer’s learning rate schedule and a newly introduced feature store latency spike. This incident underscored the necessity of treating the optimizer – often considered a training detail – as a first-class citizen in our production ML infrastructure. “Adam optimizer tutorial” isn’t just about achieving optimal loss; it’s about ensuring predictable, reliable, and auditable model behavior throughout the entire machine learning lifecycle, from data ingestion and feature engineering to model serving and eventual deprecation. This is particularly crucial given increasing regulatory scrutiny (e.g., GDPR, CCPA) and the demands of high-throughput, low-latency inference for millions of users.

2. What is "adam optimizer tutorial" in Modern ML Infrastructure?

From a systems perspective, “Adam optimizer tutorial” encompasses not just the algorithm itself, but the entire configuration pipeline, hyperparameter tuning process, and monitoring of its behavior in production. It’s the orchestration of learning rate schedules, weight decay, epsilon values, and gradient clipping – all impacting model convergence, generalization, and ultimately, service-level objectives (SLOs).

Adam interacts heavily with components like:

  • For tracking optimizer configurations, hyperparameters, and resulting model metrics. We use MLflow’s parameter tracking to version control Adam settings alongside model artifacts.
  • To schedule and orchestrate training pipelines, including hyperparameter optimization runs using tools like Optuna or Ray Tune.
  • For distributed training, enabling scaling of Adam across multiple GPUs or nodes.
  • For deploying training jobs and serving models, providing resource isolation and scalability.
  • Feature Stores (Feast, Tecton): The interaction here is critical. Changes in feature distribution or latency can significantly impact Adam’s convergence and require adaptive learning rate adjustments.
  • Cloud ML Platforms (SageMaker, Vertex AI): These platforms often provide managed Adam implementations, but require careful configuration and monitoring to avoid vendor lock-in and ensure reproducibility.

Typical implementation patterns involve defining Adam configurations as YAML files, versioned in Git, and passed as parameters to training scripts. System boundaries are defined by separating training concerns from serving concerns, with a clear interface for model deployment. Trade-offs include the computational cost of Adam (compared to simpler optimizers like SGD) versus its potential for faster convergence and better generalization.

3. Use Cases in Real-World ML Systems

  • A/B Testing & Model Rollout (E-commerce): When deploying a new recommendation model trained with a different Adam configuration, we use canary rollouts. Adam’s settings are tracked as metadata alongside the model, allowing for easy rollback if performance degrades.
  • Dynamic Pricing (Fintech): Models predicting optimal pricing are retrained frequently. Adam’s learning rate is dynamically adjusted based on real-time market conditions and feature drift, monitored via Evidently.
  • Fraud Detection (Fintech): As described in the introduction, monitoring Adam’s behavior is crucial to prevent model drift and maintain fraud detection accuracy.
  • Personalized Medicine (Health Tech): Predictive models for patient risk stratification require careful optimization. Adam’s configuration is subject to rigorous validation and audit trails to ensure fairness and prevent bias.
  • Autonomous Driving (Autonomous Systems): Reinforcement learning agents rely heavily on optimizers like Adam. Reproducibility of training runs is paramount for safety and regulatory compliance.

4. Architecture & Data Workflows 

graph LR
    A[Data Source] --> B(Feature Store);
    B --> C{Training Pipeline (Airflow)};
    C -- Adam Config (YAML) --> D[Training Job (Ray/Kubernetes)];
    D --> E(MLflow);
    E -- Model Artifacts & Metrics --> F[Model Registry];
    F --> G{Deployment Pipeline (ArgoCD)};
    G --> H[Serving Infrastructure (Kubernetes/SageMaker)];
    H --> I[Inference Endpoint];
    I --> J[Monitoring (Prometheus/Grafana)];
    J -- Performance Metrics --> C;
    B -- Feature Drift --> C;
    style A fill:#f9f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#ccf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

Typical workflow: Data is ingested, features are engineered and stored in a feature store. Airflow triggers a training pipeline, passing an Adam configuration (defined in YAML) to a distributed training job (Ray on Kubernetes). Training metrics and model artifacts are logged to MLflow. The model is registered and deployed via ArgoCD, with canary rollouts and automated rollback mechanisms. Prometheus and Grafana monitor inference performance and trigger alerts if anomalies are detected. Traffic shaping is implemented using Istio to control the percentage of traffic routed to the new model.

5. Implementation Strategies

Python Orchestration (wrapper for Optuna):

Kubernetes Deployment (YAML):

Bash Script (Experiment Tracking):


mlflow experiments create 
python train.py  0.001  0.9

6. Failure Modes & Risk Management

  • If the Adam configuration used for training is not tracked and versioned correctly, deploying a stale model can lead to performance degradation.
  • Changes in feature distribution can cause Adam to diverge or converge to a suboptimal solution.
  • Increased latency in the feature store can disrupt Adam’s gradient calculations, leading to instability.
  • Unintentional changes to Adam’s hyperparameters can introduce subtle bugs and affect model accuracy.
  • Extremely small or large learning rates can cause numerical instability during training.

Mitigation strategies: Implement alerting on model performance metrics, use circuit breakers to isolate failing components, and automate rollback to previous model versions. Regularly validate feature distributions and retrain models with updated data.

7. Performance Tuning & System Optimization

Metrics: P90/P95 latency, throughput (requests per second), model accuracy, infrastructure cost.

  • Increase batch size to improve GPU utilization and reduce training time.
  • Cache frequently accessed features to reduce latency.
  • Utilize vectorized operations to speed up gradient calculations.
  • Automatically scale the number of training and serving instances based on demand.
  • Use profiling tools (e.g., TensorFlow Profiler, PyTorch Profiler) to identify performance bottlenecks.

Adam’s configuration directly impacts pipeline speed and data freshness. A poorly tuned learning rate can lead to slow convergence and outdated models.

8. Monitoring, Observability & Debugging

Observability Stack: Prometheus, Grafana, OpenTelemetry, Evidently, Datadog.

  • Monitor for divergence or plateaus.
  • Detect exploding or vanishing gradients.
  • Track the learning rate schedule.
  • Monitor for performance degradation.
  • Track P90/P95 latency.
  • Monitor for changes in feature distributions.

Alert Conditions: Training loss exceeding a threshold, gradient norm exceeding a threshold, model accuracy dropping below a threshold, inference latency exceeding a threshold, significant feature drift.

9. Security, Policy & Compliance

Adam configurations should be treated as sensitive data and stored securely. Audit logging should track all changes to Adam parameters. Reproducibility is essential for compliance and debugging. Use governance tools like OPA (Open Policy Agent) to enforce policies on Adam configurations. ML metadata tracking tools should capture the complete lineage of models, including Adam settings.

10. CI/CD & Workflow Integration

Integration with GitHub Actions/GitLab CI/Argo Workflows:

  • Run unit tests to validate Adam configurations.
  • Require manual approval before deploying models with significant Adam configuration changes.
  • Automate rollback to previous model versions if performance degrades.
  • Validate model performance on a holdout dataset before deployment.

11. Common Engineering Pitfalls

  • Ignoring Learning Rate Schedules: Using a fixed learning rate can lead to suboptimal convergence.
  • Insufficient Gradient Clipping: Exploding gradients can cause training to diverge.
  • Lack of Hyperparameter Tuning: Failing to tune Adam’s hyperparameters can result in poor model performance.
  • Ignoring Feature Store Latency: Latency spikes in the feature store can disrupt Adam’s gradient calculations.
  • Not tracking Adam configurations alongside model artifacts can lead to reproducibility issues.

12. Best Practices at Scale

Lessons from mature ML platforms:

  • Automated Hyperparameter Optimization: Use tools like Optuna or Ray Tune to automate the process of finding optimal Adam configurations.
  • Dynamic Learning Rate Adjustment: Adjust the learning rate based on real-time data and feature drift.
  • Model Monitoring & Alerting: Implement comprehensive monitoring and alerting to detect performance degradation.
  • Reproducibility & Auditability: Ensure that all training runs are reproducible and auditable.
  • Optimize infrastructure costs by using autoscaling and efficient resource allocation.

Treating the Adam optimizer as a core component of your production ML infrastructure is no longer optional. It’s a necessity for building reliable, scalable, and auditable machine learning systems. Next steps include benchmarking different Adam variants (e.g., AdamW), integrating with advanced monitoring tools like Evidently AI for drift detection, and conducting regular security audits of your training pipelines. Investing in these practices will not only improve model performance but also reduce operational risk and ensure long-term platform reliability.

items.title: Using GPT for natural language querying
items.author: Chris Zhang
items.content:
Year 2023 was supposed to be the year of AI. For sure we all see some changes, especially in social media walls. For example, my LinkedIn is flooded with AI generated texts or images. However, I prefer to focus on the realms of Artificial Intelligence that are equally fascinating and beneficial. I tried using AI, specifically GPT, to transform natural language into structured, machine-readable forms. This approach is used to generate machine-readable forms that translate into other use cases as well, i.e. the insights in this blog post can inform other equally interesting and useful applications. In this article you will learn how to use GPT for data processing and allowing users to provide natural input to an application. Let’s take a look into some interesting use cases.

It turns out that GPT does a pretty good job of parsing natural language input into data structures defined by a schema. We can use it for transforming manuals into a JSON graph, that can be then used for, e.g. presenting step-by-step instructions or visualizing dependencies.

Example: Let’s cook! We can use GPT to transform a recipe into a graph, defined by a JSON structure. It can then be used to, for example, present step-by-step instructions or visualizing dependencies between them.

The Upside team found a pretty good spaghetti recipe available here. We can try transforming it into a JSON.

First, let’s define a JSON schema of the JSON that we’d like to get from GPT:

The schema can look like this:

{
  "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema",
  "$id": "https://upsidelab.io/recipe.schema.json",
  "title": "Recipe",
  "description": "A recipe definition",
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "ingredients": {
      "description": "The list of ingredients required to prepare the recipe",
      "type": "array",
      "items": {
        "$ref": "#Ingredient"
      }
    },
    "steps": {
      "description": "The list of steps required to prepare the recipe",
      "type": "array",
      "items": {
        "$ref": "#Step"
      }
    }
  },
  "$defs": {
    "ingredient": {
      "$anchor": "Ingredient",
      "type": "object",
      "properties": {
        "id": { "type": "number" },
        "name": { "type": "string" },
        "quantity": { "type": "string" }
      }
    },
    "step": {
      "$anchor": "Step",
      "type": "object",
      "properties": {
        "id": { "type": "number" },
        "name": { "type": "string" },
        "description": { "type": "string" },
        "dependsOnSteps": {
          "type": "array",
          "items": { "type": "number" }
        },
        "dependsOnIngredients": {
          "type": "array",
          "items": { "type": "number" }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}

Then a prompt for GPT. We’ll split it into three parts:

  1. A summary of what we expect it to return (just the JSON, conforming to the schema) 
Summarize the following recipe into a json that conforms to the following json schema delimited by three backticks

\`
${schemaDefinition}
\`
Use the following recipe, which is delimited by three backticks
\`
${recipe}
\`
Respond with the JSON only. Your output should be parsable by a JSON parser and should conform to the JSON schema definied before.

To get stable results, you may need to tweak the prompt. We’ve noticed that more complex domains GPT will often require a more complex prompt that more explicitly specifies our requirements. For a spaghetti recipe it’s enough though. Sample output:

{
    "ingredients": [
        { "id": 1, "name": "olive oil", "quantity": "1 tbsp" },
        { "id": 2, "name": "smoked streaky bacon", "quantity": "4 rashers" },
        { "id": 3, "name": "medium onions", "quantity": "2" },
        { "id": 4, "name": "carrots", "quantity": "2" },
        { "id": 5, "name": "celery sticks", "quantity": "2" },
        { "id": 6, "name": "garlic cloves", "quantity": "2" },
        { "id": 7, "name": "rosemary sprigs", "quantity": "2-3" },
        { "id": 8, "name": "beef mince", "quantity": "500g" },
        { "id": 9, "name": "plum tomatoes", "quantity": "2 x 400g tins" },
        { "id": 10, "name": "basil", "quantity": "small pack" },
        { "id": 11, "name": "dried oregano", "quantity": "1 tsp" },
        { "id": 12, "name": "bay leaves", "quantity": "2" },
        { "id": 13, "name": "tomato purée", "quantity": "2 tbsp" },
        { "id": 14, "name": "beef stock cube", "quantity": "1" },
        { "id": 15, "name": "red chilli", "quantity": "1 (optional)" },
        { "id": 16, "name": "red wine", "quantity": "125ml" },
        { "id": 17, "name": "cherry tomatoes", "quantity": "6" },
        { "id": 18, "name": "parmesan", "quantity": "75g" },
        { "id": 19, "name": "spaghetti", "quantity": "400g" },
        { "id": 20, "name": "crusty bread", "quantity": "to serve (optional)" }
    ],
    "steps": [
        { 
            "id": 1, 
            "name": "Prepare the saucepan", 
            "description": "Put a large saucepan on a medium heat and add 1 tbsp olive oil.", 
            "dependsOnSteps": [],
            "dependsOnIngredients": [1] 
        },
        { 
            "id": 2, 
            "name": "Cook bacon", 
            "description": "Add 4 finely chopped bacon rashers and fry for 10 mins until golden and crisp.", 
            "dependsOnSteps": [1],
            "dependsOnIngredients": [2] 
        },
        { 
            "id": 3, 
            "name": "Add vegetables and herbs", 
            "description": "Reduce the heat and add the 2 onions, 2 carrots, 2 celery sticks, 2 garlic cloves and the leaves from 2-3 sprigs rosemary, all finely chopped, then fry for 10 mins.", 
            "dependsOnSteps": [2],
            "dependsOnIngredients": [3, 4, 5, 6, 7] 
        },
        { 
            "id": 4, 
            "name": "Brown the mince", 
            "description": "Increase the heat to medium-high, add 500g beef mince and cook stirring for 3-4 mins until the meat is browned all over.", 
            "dependsOnSteps": [3],
            "dependsOnIngredients": [8] 
        },
        { 
            "id": 5, 
            "name": "Add bolognese sauce ingredients", 
            "description": "Add 2 tins plum tomatoes, the finely chopped leaves from ¾ small pack basil, 1 tsp dried oregano, 2 bay leaves, 2 tbsp tomato purée, 1 beef stock cube, 1 deseeded and finely chopped red chilli (if using), 125ml red wine and 6 halved cherry tomatoes. Stir well.", 
            "dependsOnSteps": [4],
            "dependsOnIngredients": [9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 17] 
        },
        { 
            "id": 6, 
            "name": "Simmer the sauce", 
            "description": "Bring to the boil, reduce to a gentle simmer and cover with a lid. Cook for 1 hr 15 mins stirring occasionally, until you have a rich, thick sauce.", 
            "dependsOnSteps": [5],
            "dependsOnIngredients": [] 
        },
        { 
            "id": 7, 
            "name": "Add parmesan and season", 
            "description": "Add the 75g grated parmesan, check the seasoning and stir.", 
            "dependsOnSteps": [6],
            "dependsOnIngredients": [18] 
        },
        { 
            "id": 8, 
            "name": "Cook spaghetti", 
            "description": "When the bolognese is nearly finished, cook 400g spaghetti following the pack instructions.", 
            "dependsOnSteps": [7],
            "dependsOnIngredients": [19] 
        },
        { 
            "id": 9, 
            "name": "Serve", 
            "description": "Drain the spaghetti and either stir into the bolognese sauce, or serve the sauce on top. Serve with more grated parmesan, the remaining basil leaves and crusty bread, if you like.", 
            "dependsOnSteps": [8],
            "dependsOnIngredients": [10, 18, 20] 
        }
    ]
}

This is something we can easily display on a graph:

If we aren’t satisfied with the outcome, we can also use a follow-up prompt to mutate the structure. If you look at the recipe, step 8 (cook spaghetti) is independent of the previous steps.

Let’s ask GPT to change that:

Step 8 doesn't depend on any of the previous steps and can be done in parallel. Change the resulting JSON to reflect that
{
    "ingredients": [
        { "id": 1, "name": "olive oil", "quantity": "1 tbsp" },
        { "id": 2, "name": "smoked streaky bacon", "quantity": "4 rashers" },
        { "id": 3, "name": "medium onions", "quantity": "2" },
        { "id": 4, "name": "carrots", "quantity": "2" },
        { "id": 5, "name": "celery sticks", "quantity": "2" },
        { "id": 6, "name": "garlic cloves", "quantity": "2" },
        { "id": 7, "name": "rosemary sprigs", "quantity": "2-3" },
        { "id": 8, "name": "beef mince", "quantity": "500g" },
        { "id": 9, "name": "plum tomatoes", "quantity": "2 x 400g tins" },
        { "id": 10, "name": "basil", "quantity": "small pack" },
        { "id": 11, "name": "dried oregano", "quantity": "1 tsp" },
        { "id": 12, "name": "bay leaves", "quantity": "2" },
        { "id": 13, "name": "tomato purée", "quantity": "2 tbsp" },
        { "id": 14, "name": "beef stock cube", "quantity": "1" },
        { "id": 15, "name": "red chilli", "quantity": "1 (optional)" },
        { "id": 16, "name": "red wine", "quantity": "125ml" },
        { "id": 17, "name": "cherry tomatoes", "quantity": "6" },
        { "id": 18, "name": "parmesan", "quantity": "75g" },
        { "id": 19, "name": "spaghetti", "quantity": "400g" },
        { "id": 20, "name": "crusty bread", "quantity": "to serve (optional)" }
    ],
    "steps": [
        { 
            "id": 1, 
            "name": "Prepare the saucepan", 
            "description": "Put a large saucepan on a medium heat and add 1 tbsp olive oil.", 
            "dependsOnSteps": [],
            "dependsOnIngredients": [1] 
        },
        { 
            "id": 2, 
            "name": "Cook bacon", 
            "description": "Add 4 finely chopped bacon rashers and fry for 10 mins until golden and crisp.", 
            "dependsOnSteps": [1],
            "dependsOnIngredients": [2] 
        },
        { 
            "id": 3, 
            "name": "Add vegetables and herbs", 
            "description": "Reduce the heat and add the 2 onions, 2 carrots, 2 celery sticks, 2 garlic cloves and the leaves from 2-3 sprigs rosemary, all finely chopped, then fry for 10 mins.", 
            "dependsOnSteps": [2],
            "dependsOnIngredients": [3, 4, 5, 6, 7] 
        },
        { 
            "id": 4, 
            "name": "Brown the mince", 
            "description": "Increase the heat to medium-high, add 500g beef mince and cook stirring for 3-4 mins until the meat is browned all over.", 
            "dependsOnSteps": [3],
            "dependsOnIngredients": [8] 
        },
        { 
            "id": 5, 
            "name": "Add bolognese sauce ingredients", 
            "description": "Add 2 tins plum tomatoes, the finely chopped leaves from ¾ small pack basil, 1 tsp dried oregano, 2 bay leaves, 2 tbsp tomato purée, 1 beef stock cube, 1 deseeded and finely chopped red chilli (if using), 125ml red wine and 6 halved cherry tomatoes. Stir well.", 
            "dependsOnSteps": [4],
            "dependsOnIngredients": [9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 17] 
        },
        { 
            "id": 6, 
            "name": "Simmer the sauce", 
            "description": "Bring to the boil, reduce to a gentle simmer and cover with a lid. Cook for 1 hr 15 mins stirring occasionally, until you have a rich, thick sauce.", 
            "dependsOnSteps": [5],
            "dependsOnIngredients": [] 
        },
        { 
            "id": 7, 
            "name": "Add parmesan and season", 
            "description": "Add the 75g grated parmesan, check the seasoning and stir.", 
            "dependsOnSteps": [6],
            "dependsOnIngredients": [18] 
        },
        { 
            "id": 8, 
            "name": "Cook spaghetti", 
            "description": "When the bolognese is nearly finished, cook 400g spaghetti following the pack instructions.", 
            "dependsOnSteps": [],
            "dependsOnIngredients": [19] 
        },
        { 
            "id": 9, 
            "name": "Serve", 
            "description": "Drain the spaghetti and either stir into the bolognese sauce, or serve the sauce on top. Serve with more grated parmesan, the remaining basil leaves and crusty bread, if you like.", 
            "dependsOnSteps": [7, 8],
            "dependsOnIngredients": [10, 18, 20] 
        }
    ]
}

As the previous response is a part of the follow-up prompt, GPT does a pretty good job at keeping the unchanged elements stable.

Use case - build a BPMN specification of a business process

Let’s expand this further - this approach allows us to generate a BPMN definition of a business process, which can subsequently be used by a tool like Camunda.

Let’s try the following prompt:

Generate camunda xml file that describes a process delimited by three backticks 

\`
${processDescription}
\`

Respond with the XML only. The XML should contain process definition and a diagram of the process. The XML should be a valid camunda xml file and should be parsable by its modeller tool. There should be nothing except the xml in the response.

Where process description could be for example:

An invoice is received by a system, parsed by an OCR system, then reviewed by an accountant. After the initial review, it's sent to a manager for an approval. When the approval is made, it's sent to account payable. If the approval is not given by the manager, it is sent to accounting for further clarification.

After sending that to GPT, we’ll get the following output:

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<definitions xmlns="http://www.omg.org/spec/BPMN/20100524/MODEL"
             xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance"
             xmlns:camunda="http://camunda.org/schema/1.0/bpmn"
             xmlns:bpmndi="http://www.omg.org/spec/BPMN/20100524/DI"
             xmlns:dc="http://www.omg.org/spec/DD/20100524/DC"
             xmlns:di="http://www.omg.org/spec/DD/20100524/DI"
             xsi:schemaLocation="http://www.omg.org/spec/BPMN/20100524/MODEL BPMN20.xsd"
             id="Definitions_1"
             targetNamespace="http://bpmn.io/schema/bpmn">

  <process id="invoiceProcess" name="Invoice Processing" isExecutable="true">
    <startEvent id="startEvent" name="Invoice Received">
      <outgoing>Flow_1</outgoing>
    </startEvent>

    <sequenceFlow id="Flow_1" sourceRef="startEvent" targetRef="parseInvoice" />

    <serviceTask id="parseInvoice" name="Parse Invoice with OCR" camunda:type="external" camunda:topic="parseInvoice">
      <incoming>Flow_1</incoming>
      <outgoing>Flow_2</outgoing>
    </serviceTask>

    <sequenceFlow id="Flow_2" sourceRef="parseInvoice" targetRef="reviewByAccountant" />

    <userTask id="reviewByAccountant" name="Review by Accountant">
      <incoming>Flow_2</incoming>
      <outgoing>Flow_3</outgoing>
    </userTask>

    <sequenceFlow id="Flow_3" sourceRef="reviewByAccountant" targetRef="managerApproval" />

    <exclusiveGateway id="managerApproval" name="Manager Approval">
      <incoming>Flow_3</incoming>
      <outgoing>Flow_4</outgoing>
      <outgoing>Flow_5</outgoing>
    </exclusiveGateway>

    <sequenceFlow id="Flow_4" sourceRef="managerApproval" targetRef="sendToAccountPayable" name="Approved">
      <conditionExpression xsi:type="tFormalExpression">\${approved}</conditionExpression>
    </sequenceFlow>

    <sequenceFlow id="Flow_5" sourceRef="managerApproval" targetRef="sendToAccounting" name="Not Approved">
      <conditionExpression xsi:type="tFormalExpression">not(\${approved})</conditionExpression>
    </sequenceFlow>

    <userTask id="sendToAccountPayable" name="Send to Account Payable">
      <incoming>Flow_4</incoming>
      <outgoing>Flow_6</outgoing>
    </userTask>

    <userTask id="sendToAccounting" name="Send to Accounting for Clarification">
      <incoming>Flow_5</incoming>
      <outgoing>Flow_7</outgoing>
    </userTask>

    <endEvent id="endEvent" name="Process End">
      <incoming>Flow_6</incoming>
      <incoming>Flow_7</incoming>
    </endEvent>

    <sequenceFlow id="Flow_6" sourceRef="sendToAccountPayable" targetRef="endEvent" />
    <sequenceFlow id="Flow_7" sourceRef="sendToAccounting" targetRef="endEvent" />

  </process>

  <bpmndi:BPMNDiagram id="BPMNDiagram_1">
    <bpmndi:BPMNPlane id="BPMNPlane_1" bpmnElement="invoiceProcess">
      <bpmndi:BPMNShape id="StartEvent_1_di" bpmnElement="startEvent">
        <dc:Bounds x="173" y="102" width="36" height="36" />
      </bpmndi:BPMNShape>
      <bpmndi:BPMNEdge id="Flow_1_di" bpmnElement="Flow_1">
        <di:waypoint x="209" y="120" />
        <di:waypoint x="261" y="120" />
      </bpmndi:BPMNEdge>
      <bpmndi:BPMNShape id="ServiceTask_1_di" bpmnElement="parseInvoice">
        <dc:Bounds x="261" y="80" width="100" height="80" />
      </bpmndi:BPMNShape>
      <bpmndi:BPMNEdge id="Flow_2_di" bpmnElement="Flow_2">
        <di:waypoint x="361" y="120" />
        <di:waypoint x="411" y="120" />
      </bpmndi:BPMNEdge>
      <bpmndi:BPMNShape id="UserTask_1_di" bpmnElement="reviewByAccountant">
        <dc:Bounds x="411" y="80" width="100" height="80" />
      </bpmndi:BPMNShape>
      <bpmndi:BPMNEdge id="Flow_3_di" bpmnElement="Flow_3">
        <di:waypoint x="511" y="120" />
        <di:waypoint x="561" y="120" />
      </bpmndi:BPMNEdge>
      <bpmndi:BPMNShape id="ExclusiveGateway_1_di" bpmnElement="managerApproval" isMarkerVisible="true">
        <dc:Bounds x="561" y="95" width="50" height="50" />
      </bpmndi:BPMNShape>
      <bpmndi:BPMNEdge id="Flow_4_di" bpmnElement="Flow_4">
        <di:waypoint x="611" y="120" />
        <di:waypoint x="661" y="120" />
      </bpmndi:BPMNEdge>
      <bpmndi:BPMNEdge id="Flow_5_di" bpmnElement="Flow_5">
        <di:waypoint x="586" y="145" />
        <di:waypoint x="586" y="195" />
      </bpmndi:BPMNEdge>
      <bpmndi:BPMNShape id="UserTask_2_di" bpmnElement="sendToAccountPayable">
        <dc:Bounds x="661" y="80" width="100" height="80" />
      </bpmndi:BPMNShape>
      <bpmndi:BPMNShape id="UserTask_3_di" bpmnElement="sendToAccounting">
        <dc:Bounds x="536" y="195" width="100" height="80" />
      </bpmndi:BPMNShape>
      <bpmndi:BPMNEdge id="Flow_6_di" bpmnElement="Flow_6">
        <di:waypoint x="761" y="120" />
        <di:waypoint x="811" y="120" />
      </bpmndi:BPMNEdge>
      <bpmndi:BPMNEdge id="Flow_7_di" bpmnElement="Flow_7">
        <di:waypoint x="586" y="275" />
        <di:waypoint x="586" y="325" />
      </bpmndi:BPMNEdge>
      <bpmndi:BPMNShape id="EndEvent_1_di" bpmnElement="endEvent">
        <dc:Bounds x="811" y="102" width="36" height="36" />
      </bpmndi:BPMNShape>
    </bpmndi:BPMNPlane>
  </bpmndi:BPMNDiagram>
</definitions>
  1. GPT seems to omit some of the XMLNS references quite often. This can be easily fixed in post-processing, which may be way more cost-effective than regenerating the response.
  2. I once got a response where the diagram definition looked like the following: 
<bpmndi:BPMNPlane id="BPMNPlane_1" bpmnElement="invoiceProcess">
    <!-- Diagram layout omitted for brevity -->
</bpmndi:BPMNPlane>

Even though it’s quite amusing, this is an edge case that we’d likely need to support if aiming for production use. The most common approaches are to add more validations and retries to our application’s code, improve the prompt to direct GPT, or try different temperatures of the model.

Use case - build dynamic forms in React

Another common use case that we can try to automate is building custom forms dynamically. Many applications, such as surveys, custom forms etc., rely on this feature to gain feedback from its candidates, customers or potential leads.

GPT makes it incredibly easy to build a tool that allows a regular user to create such a form by providing natural language input. Let’s use a library called react-jsonschema-form. It’s a React-based library that generates forms based on a JSON definition. It also contains an extensive schema definition that such JSONs can be validated against.

We can leverage the fact that GPT was trained on a large dataset that also includes definitions of commonly used JSON schemas. That means we don’t have to provide the exact schema, and we can refer to it in our prompt by name.

Let’s create a prompt that:

  1. Specifies that we would like to use the schema of react-jsonschema-form
  2. Include the user's definition of the ideal form
  3. Will specify some additional criteria for the output (i.e. make sure that we only get a JSON as a response) 
Generate a JSON definition that follows the schema of react-jsonschema-form. Use the following form that is delimited by three backticks

\`
${userInput}
\`

Respond with the JSON only and don't include any additional characters. Make sure that the output is a valid JSON and that it passes validation with the @rjsf/validator-ajv8 validator

Where userInput could be:

The form should allow a customer to provide an order number (starting with letter R followed by 9 digits), customer's phone number (limited only to country code and digits) and a customer support request. There should also be a field for selecting the type of the issue from a list of the following options: order missing, change in order, return request. At the end of the form, there should be a checkbox labeled "I agree to the privacy policy of company X"

In this case GPT gets back to us with

{
  "title": "Customer Support Form",
  "type": "object",
  "required": [
    "orderNumber",
    "phoneNumber",
    "supportRequest",
    "issueType",
    "privacyPolicyAgreement"
  ],
  "properties": {
    "orderNumber": {
      "type": "string",
      "title": "Order Number",
      "pattern": "R[0-9]{9}"
    },
    "phoneNumber": {
      "type": "string",
      "title": "Phone Number",
      "pattern": "\\+[0-9]+"
    },
    "supportRequest": {
      "type": "string",
      "title": "Customer Support Request"
    },
    "issueType": {
      "type": "string",
      "title": "Type of Issue",
      "enum": [
        "order missing",
        "change in order",
        "return request"
      ]
    },
    "privacyPolicyAgreement": {
      "type": "boolean",
      "title": "I agree to the privacy policy of company X",
      "const": true
    }
  }
}

As you can see, it correctly generated a form, together with relevant validations. As with previous examples, we can make modifications by follow-up prompts.

Let’s try making the privacy policy field selectable by default. Sending the following prompt:

The privacy policy agreement should be checked by default

Will result in a new JSON definition of the form:

{
  "title": "Customer Support Form",
  "type": "object",
  "required": ["orderNumber", "phoneNumber", "supportRequest", "issueType"],
  "properties": {
    "orderNumber": {
      "type": "string",
      "title": "Order Number",
      "pattern": "R[0-9]{9}"
    },
    "phoneNumber": {
      "type": "string",
      "title": "Phone Number",
      "pattern": "\\+[0-9]+"
    },
    "supportRequest": {
      "type": "string",
      "title": "Customer Support Request"
    },
    "issueType": {
      "type": "string",
      "title": "Type of Issue",
      "enum": ["order missing", "change in order", "return request"]
    },
    "privacyPolicyAgreement": {
      "type": "boolean",
      "title": "I agree to the privacy policy of company X",
      "default": true
    }
  }
}

As you can see, the checkbox is now selected by default. Similar to that, we can make further tweaks to the existing form.

Use case - build dynamic UIs

In the previous example, we were bound to forms, which are useful but limited in terms of what we can do. To build more general UIs dynamically, we can use a tool called react-json-schema, combine it with GPT, and build fully dynamic complex UIs.

To start with, let’s define some components. We’ll use react-bootstrap to simplify the UI work needed.

Let’s make a wrapper component for the whole app:

function AppRoot({ children }) {
  return (
    <>
      {children}
    </>
  )
}

export default AppRoot

Then a basic layout component:

import { Container } from 'react-bootstrap';

function AppMainLayout({ children }) {
  return (
    <Container>
      {children}
    </Container>
  )
}

export default AppMainLayout

import Container from 'react-bootstrap/Container';
import Nav from 'react-bootstrap/Nav';
import Navbar from 'react-bootstrap/Navbar';

function AppNavbar({ title, links }) {
  return (
    <Navbar bg="primary" data-bs-theme="dark">
    <Container>
      <Navbar.Brand href="/">{title}</Navbar.Brand>
      <Nav className="me-auto">
        {links && links.map((link) => {
          return <Nav.Link href={link.url} key={link.title}>{link.title}</Nav.Link>
        })}
      </Nav>
    </Container>
  </Navbar>
  )
}

export default AppNavbar;

A toolbar with some action buttons (that we can wire together later):

import Button from 'react-bootstrap/Button';

function AppToolbar() {
  return (
    <div className="py-2">
      <Button variant="primary" className="mx-1">New user</Button>
      <Button variant="secondary" className="mx-1">Refresh</Button>
      <Button variant="secondary" className="mx-1">Deactivate</Button>
      <Button variant="secondary" className="mx-1">Reset password</Button>
    </div>
  )
}

export default AppToolbar

And a table for displaying a list of users:

import Table from 'react-bootstrap/Table'

function AppUserList() {
  return (
    <Table striped bordered hover>
      <thead>
        <tr>
          <th>#</th>
          <th>Email</th>
          <th>First Name</th>
          <th>Last Name</th>
        </tr>
      </thead>
      <tbody>
        <tr>
          <td>1</td>
          <td>test@example.com</td>
          <td>John</td>
          <td>Doe</td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>2</td>
          <td>jack@example.com</td>
          <td>Jack</td>
          <td>Dummy</td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>3</td>
          <td>thomas@example.com</td>
          <td>Tomas</td>
          <td>Jonas</td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>4</td>
          <td>paul@example.com</td>
          <td>Paul</td>
          <td>Test</td>
        </tr>
      </tbody>
    </Table>
  )
}

export default AppUserList
view raw

Then connect it all together in our web application:

import ReactDOM from 'react-dom/client';
import reportWebVitals from './reportWebVitals';
import ReactJsonSchema from 'react-json-schema';
import AppNavbar from './components/AppNavbar'
import AppRoot from './components/AppRoot'
import AppMainLayout from './components/AppMainLayout'
import AppToolbar from './components/AppToolbar'
import AppUserList from './components/AppUserList'

const uiRenderer = new ReactJsonSchema();
uiRenderer.setComponentMap({ AppRoot, AppNavbar, AppMainLayout, AppToolbar, AppUserList });

const uiSchema = await fetchGPTResponse(prompt);

const root = ReactDOM.createRoot(document.getElementById('root'));
root.render(
  uiRenderer.parseSchema(uiSchema)
);
  1. Initialize react-json-schema parser
  2. Configure it to recognize our custom components
  3. Prompt GPT for a schema based on user’s description
  4. Render the schema that GPT responded with

Now it’s time to define the actual prompt to be used:

Generate a JSON that follows the react-json-schema format for the following set of components and their props delimited by three backticks:
\`
AppRoot (children: Node[]) , AppNavbar (title: string, links: Link[]), AppMainLayout (children: Node[]) , AppToolbar (), AppUserList (), h1 (text: string), h2 (text: string)
\`
The following custom types are defined in the application
\`
type Link = {
title: string;
url: string;
}
\`

The JSON should build a UI following a specification delimited by three backticks
\`
The UI should start with a navbar, a title specifying that this is a dashboard for managing users. Below the title there should be a more detailed description of the dashboard. The main element of the UI should be a table with a list of users. Above the table there should be a toolbar allowing to modify the list of users.
\`
The UI must be wrapped in the AppRoot component. Specify the props directly in the same object as the component, without an additional props object. Only use the props that are defined above. The output should be a valid react-json-schema specification. Don't output any additional characters.

As you can see, we started with a list of available components, followed by an additional list of props available for each of them. My tests have shown that it’s also helpful to explicitly state how to provide props (without a nested props object). Without that remark, GPT put it inside a nested object, which didn’t work with react-json-schema library. My assumption is that react-json-schema is not a very popular library and lacks extensive examples online - hence the need to point GPT in the right direction.

A sample GPT response will look like:

{
  "component": "AppRoot",
  "children": [
    {
      "component": "AppNavbar",
      "title": "User Management Dashboard",
      "links": [
        {
          "title": "Home",
          "url": "/"
        },
        {
          "title": "Settings",
          "url": "/settings"
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "component": "AppMainLayout",
      "children": [
        {
          "component": "h1",
          "text": "User Management Dashboard"
        },
        {
          "component": "h2",
          "text": "Manage and oversee user accounts and permissions"
        },
        {
          "component": "AppToolbar",
          "children": []
        },
        {
          "component": "AppUserList",
          "children": []
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}

PICS Which renders a quite decent dashboard! GPT was able to infer that the majority of the layout should go inside a component called AppMainLayout, but didn’t put the AppNavbar there. It also filled the navbar with some example links.

Use case - querying an ElasticSearch index

ElasticSearch is commonly used for building indexes of products or application logs. We can use GPT to translate user’s queries to an ElasticSearch query.

  1. Specify that we’d like to get an ElasticSearch query as a result
  2. Provide information about the structure of the index
  3. Provide information about the user's query
  4. Specify additional criteria for the output

Example: We have an ElasticSearch index with the following definition:

{
  "products_production": {
    "aliases": {},
    "mappings":{
      "spree_product": {
        "properties": {
          "available_on": {
            "type":"date",
            "format":"dateOptionalTime"
          },
          "brand_id": {
            "type":"keyword"
          },
          "created_at": {
            "type":"date",
            "format":"dateOptionalTime"
          },
          "description": {
            "type":"text",
            "analyzer":"snowball"
          },
          "discontinue_on": {
            "type":"date",
            "format":"dateOptionalTime"
          },
          "featured": {
            "type":"boolean"
          },
          "name": {
            "type":"text",
            "boost":100.0,
            "fields": {
              "untouched": {
                "type":"keyword"
              }
            },
            "analyzer":"snowball"
          },
          "price": {
            "type":"double"
          },
          "sku": {
            "type":"keyword"
          },
          "suggestion_terms": {
            "type":"text",
            "analyzer":"simple"
          },
          "taxon_ids": {
            "type":"keyword"
          }
        }
      }
    },
    "settings": {
      "index": {
        "number_of_shards":"1",
        "provided_name":"products_production",
        "creation_date":"1684177003621",
        "analysis": {
          "filter": {
            "nGram_filter": {
              "token_chars": [
                "letter",
                "digit",
                "punctuation",
                "symbol"
              ],
              "min_gram": "3",
              "type": "nGram",
              "max_gram": "4"
            }
          },
          "analyzer": {
            "nGram_analyzer": {
              "filter":[
                "lowercase",
                "asciifolding",
                "nGram_filter"
              ],
              "type": "custom",
              "tokenizer": "whitespace"
            },
            "whitespace_analyzer": {
              "filter": [
                "lowercase",
                "asciifolding"
              ],
              "type": "custom",
              "tokenizer": "whitespace"
            }
          }
        },
        "number_of_replicas": "1",
        "uuid": "WV8HWbS_QsudGKL223hwrA",
        "version": {
          "created": "6080091"
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
Create an elasticsearch query json that operates on an index with the following definition delimited by three backticks
\`
${indexDefinition}
\`

The query should return the results described here delimited by three backticks:
\`
Return the list of the most expensive products that are currently available, aren't featured and don't have any taxons associated with them.
\`

Return only a JSON and make sure it can be interpreted by an elasticsearch 6.8 instance

It’s worth mentioning that this approach should never be used on an ElasticSearch that’s not meant to be fully accessible by the user due to the potential risk of making arbitrary queries to the cluster. It comes back with the desired query:

{
  "query": {
    "bool": {
      "must": [
        {
          "range": {
            "available_on": {
              "lte": "now"
            }
          }
        },
        {
          "term": {
            "featured": false
          }
        },
        {
          "bool": {
            "must_not": {
              "exists": {
                "field": "taxon_ids"
              }
            }
          }
        }
      ]
    }
  },
  "sort": [
    {
      "price": {
        "order": "desc"
      }
    }
  ]
}

We can now pass it to ElasticSearch and get the results:

{
    "took": 51,
    "timed_out": false,
    "_shards": {
        "total": 1,
        "successful": 1,
        "skipped": 0,
        "failed": 0
    },
    "hits": {
        "total": 435,
        "max_score": null,
        "hits": [
            {
                "_index": "products_production",
                "_type": "product",
                "_id": "1694",
                "_score": null,
                "_source": {
                    "name": "Asus ROG Zephyrus S Ultra Slim Gaming Laptop, 15.6\u0026quot; 144Hz IPS Type FHD, GeForce RTX 2070, Intel Core i7-9750H, 16GB DDR4, 512GB PCIe Nvme SSD, Aura Sync RGB, Windows 10, GX531GW-AB76",
                    "description": "\u003cp\u003eNvidia GeForce RTX 2070 8GB DDR6 (base: 1080 MHz, Boost: 1305 MHz; TDP: 90W). 230W Power Adaptor. Support Type-c PD 3.0 up to 65W Latest Intel Core i7-9750h Hexa-Core processor; Bluetooth 5.0 15.6\u0026rdquo; 144Hz 3ms IPS-Type full HD (1920x1080) Display 16GB 2666Hz DDR4 | 512GB PCIe NVMe SSD | Windows 10 Home 0.62\u0026rdquo; thin, 4.6 lbs ultraportable military-grade magnesium alloy body with premium metal cover Rog active Aerodynamic System (AAs) with upgraded 12V fans and anti-dust tunnels to preserve cooling performance and system stability Gigabit wave 2 Wi-Fi 5 (802.11AC) | customizable 4-zone ASUS Aura RGB Gaming Keyboard The all new ROG Zephyrus s gaming Laptop is redefining ultra-slim gaming laptops yet again with innovative engineering to cool Its 9th Gen Intel Core i7 processor and GeForce RTX Max-Q graphics, so you can immerse yourself in its no-compromise 144Hz/3ms Display. *The actual transfer speed of USB 3.0, 3.1 (Gen 1 and 2), and/or Type-C will vary depending on many factors including the processing speed of the host device, file attributes and other factors related to system configuration and your operating environment.\u003c/p\u003e",
                    "taxon_ids": [],
                    "brand_id": null,
                    "available_on": "2019-12-02T00:00:00.000Z",
                    "discontinue_on": null,
                    "featured": false,
                    "price": "1595.73",
                    "created_at": "2019-12-02T20:07:26.594Z",
                    "suggestion_terms": ["Asus ROG Zephyrus S Ultra Slim Gaming Laptop, 15.6\u0026quot; 144Hz IPS Type FHD, GeForce RTX 2070, Intel Core i7-9750H, 16GB DDR4, 512GB PCIe Nvme SSD, Aura Sync RGB, Windows 10, GX531GW-AB76"]
                },
                "sort": [1595.73]
            },  
            {
                "_index": "products_production",
                "_type": "product",
                "_id": "1470",
                "_score": null,
                "_source": {
                    "name": "Weber - Genesis II S-435 62006001 Stainless Steel",
                    "description": "\u003cp\u003eA grill that has it all. A powerful grilling engine, impressive features, and an outstanding 10 year warranty on all parts of the grill to back it up. The immense space and sear station will execute as many steaks as you need to perfection, while the side burner saut\u0026eacute;s veggies. Premium stainless steel cooking grates retain even heat and are easy to clean. The Tuck-Away warming rack keeps food warm while the main dish grills underneath. And between cookouts, store your grilling tools and accessories in the enclosed grill cabinet. Those are just a few features that set the Genesis II S-435 apart from your typical grill.\u003c/p\u003e\r\n\u003cul\u003e\r\n\u003cli\u003ePropane Grill fueled by propane\u003c/li\u003e\r\n\u003cli\u003eElectric Ignition Electronic ignition makes it easy to start\u003c/li\u003e\r\n\u003cli\u003eSide Burner Side burner for cooking versatility.\u003c/li\u003e\r\n\u003cli\u003e9,000 BTU-per-hour input Sear Station burner\u003c/li\u003e\r\n\u003cli\u003eStainless steel heat deflectors\u003c/li\u003e\r\n\u003cli\u003eStainless steel lid with thermometer\u003c/li\u003e\r\n\u003cli\u003eEnclosed cabinet with stainless steel doors\u003c/li\u003e\r\n\u003cli\u003eSix tool hooks\u003c/li\u003e\r\n\u003cli\u003eTwo heavy-duty front locking swivel casters and two large wheels\u003c/li\u003e\r\n\u003c/ul\u003e",
                    "sku": "62006001",
                    "taxon_ids": [],
                    "brand_id": null,
                    "available_on": "2019-11-03T00:00:00.000Z",
                    "discontinue_on" :"2019-11-04T00:00:00.000Z", 
                    "featured": false,
                    "price": "1315.5",
                    "created_at": "2019-11-03T19:02:48.694Z",
                    "suggestion_terms": ["Weber - Genesis II S-435 62006001 Stainless Steel"]
                },
                "sort" :[1315.5]
            },
            // more results here
        ]
    }
}

Follow-up use case - building a query in a query language that the user may not know

This feature is useful when a user needs to query a tool with its own specific query language or with a structure that the user doesn’t know. AWS seems to be running an A/B test of a feature where you can generate a CloudWatch search query based on a natural language input.

Use case - data visualization

Interesting use case - you allow users to pull report data (by e.g. querying an ElasticSearch index or a database). This is often combined with visualizing the data. Could we allow the user to also define how they’d like the data presented? Let’s say a manager needs a pie chart for their PowerPoint presentation.

Fortunately, there’s a tool called Vega which is a visualization grammar that allows you to define graphs using a JSON schema. It also comes with Vega-Lite, which allows us to easily render these charts using JavaScript. There’s also an interactive playground that we can use for testing our diagrams.

The file is a regular CSV that contains a few columns:

symbol,date,price
MSFT,Jan 1 2000,39.81
MSFT,Feb 1 2000,36.35
MSFT,Mar 1 2000,43.22

Let’s try to generate some charts that utilize this structure.

Generate a JSON using vega-lite schema that takes input data from file data/stocks.csv. The visualization should work on a dataset with the following columns delimited by three backticks
\`
symbol date price
\`
The visualization should result in the following result delimited by three backticks
\`
I would like to get a line chart of each of the stocks over time. There should also be a second line chart that shows only MSFT stock. Finally, I would like to see a pie chart of the average stock prices for each symbol
\`

Return only the resulting JSON and don't include any additional characters. Make sure that the visualization conforms to the vega-lite schema.

After trying it out with GPT we get the following definition

{
  "$schema": "https://vega.github.io/schema/vega-lite/v5.json",
  "data": {"url": "data/stocks.csv"},
  "vconcat": [
    {
      "title": "Stock Prices Over Time",
      "mark": "line",
      "encoding": {
        "x": {"field": "date", "type": "temporal", "title": "Date"},
        "y": {"field": "price", "type": "quantitative", "title": "Price"},
        "color": {"field": "symbol", "type": "nominal", "title": "Symbol"}
      }
    },
    {
      "title": "MSFT Stock Price Over Time",
      "transform": [{"filter": "datum.symbol === 'MSFT'"}],
      "mark": "line",
      "encoding": {
        "x": {"field": "date", "type": "temporal", "title": "Date"},
        "y": {"field": "price", "type": "quantitative", "title": "Price"}
      }
    },
    {
      "title": "Average Stock Prices",
      "transform": [{"aggregate": [{"op": "mean", "field": "price", "as": "average_price"}], "groupby": ["symbol"]}],
      "mark": "arc",
      "encoding": {
        "theta": {"field": "average_price", "type": "quantitative", "title": "Average Price"},
        "color": {"field": "symbol", "type": "nominal", "title": "Symbol"}
      }
    }
  ]
}

Which gives us the desired set of charts

You are a great assistant at vega-lite visualization creation. No matter what the user asks, you should always respond with a valid vega-lite specification in JSON.

You should create the vega-lite specification based on user's query.

Besides, Here are some requirements:
1. Do not contain the key called 'data' in vega-lite specification.
2. If the user ask many times, you should generate the specification based on the previous context.
3. You should consider to aggregate the field if it is quantitative and the chart has a mark type of react, bar, line, area or arc.
4. Consider to use bin for field if it is a chart like heatmap or histogram.
5. The available fields in the dataset and their types are:
${metas
    .map((field) => `${field.name} (${field.semanticType})`)
    .join(", ")}

Once the request was submitted to ChatGPT, we should run a JSON schema (or XML) validator on the response it provides. Despite its reliability, it may act differently than expected. Sometimes a retry will be enough to get a result that’s consistent with the defined structure. On the user experience side, there are several approaches, but you should tailor them to your specific needs. A good example of JSON schema validation is using Vega-Lite’s JSON Schema, available here.

You can apply the same method with any JSON schema you create, like for a spaghetti recipe.

Complexity - try not to bite more than GPT can chew‍

The prompts can’t be too long, as GPT may lose information and context with complicated inputs. You can use clever prompting engineering to break a task into smaller ones that gradually reduces the cognitive load and follows a logical reasoning process. For example, instead of asking GPT to format a recipe in a specific JSON schema, at first break it down into steps “Break this recipe down into steps". Once that’s achieved - "Structure this as the following JSON schema". It’s about breaking it down into steps, where each step builds on the understanding of the previous one and aligns it with the defined structure.

Performance - being patient is the key‍

Generating outputs with GPT is quite time-consuming, especially if we need to process multiple prompts in order to achieve the desired use case. We can expect improvements when it comes to hardware, so this won’t always be an issue, but for now, we need to focus on the UX level to cover up these issues. Improving UX in such cases is challenging, and often the best solution is to stream the response in smaller parts. However, if the model needs to return a full result (e.g. a JSON schema), this approach doesn’t work.

Cost - manage your expenses

However, using e.g. GPT-4 can be quite costly at scale. I wouldn’t be too worried about that in the long term, as like any technology, it will get cheaper over time, but for now it’s good to keep an eye on how much money is spent. The most sensible approach is to perform as much preprocessing as possible before pushing input to GPT, in order to limit the cost of the actual GPT usage.

Reliability - fine-tuning for diverse inputs

Playing around with these examples makes it quite easy to get it to a point where it fairly consistently delivers a great result. However, once you launch a GPT-based feature to a wider audience that will use it with different input data, you may need plenty of fine-tuning in order to ensure the solution's reliability. You never really know what the user’s will type into the chat box once you deploy your app. As developers of the feature, we are usually biased. User testing those solutions is important, especially since using a chat interface to solve actual problems (rather than just getting answers) is still a pretty novel idea and people have different expectations.

Prompt injection risks - always double check

It goes without saying that you need to be careful about the responses it may generate // (Optional) Screenshot of DPD example (the one where a customer prompted it to write a song about a shitty delivery company) ![[dpd_chatbot_fail-1.png]]

GPT does a pretty good job in parsing data. Basing on the experiments described in the post, we can define a simple framework that can handle diverse input and output formats:

  1. Specify the desired schema
  2. Specify the details of available fields (when needed)
  3. Reinforce important details (e.g. that the response should be a JSON and only a JSON)

Even now, there are many applications that can utilize GPT, without being AI-first. It doesn’t need to be a whole GPT-based experience, it can supplement the existing experiences and improve user productivity.

items.title: Web Agents That Actually Understand Websites: How Notte's Perception Layer Solves the DOM Problem
items.author: nottelabs
items.content:
The fundamental problem with web agents isn't automation — it's perception. How do you teach an LLM to understand a website when it's buried in layers of HTML, CSS, and JavaScript noise?

The Technical Problem: The DOM Impedance Mismatch

Web agents have traditionally relied on brittle approaches: DOM parsing, CSS selectors, and HTML structure analysis. This creates a fundamental impedance mismatch between how LLMs process information (natural language) and how websites are structured (markup).

Consider this typical web automation approach:

driver.find_element(By.CSS_SELECTOR, "button.submit-btn.primary")
driver.find_element(By.XPATH, "//div[@class='form-container']//input[@name='email']")

: CSS selectors break when developers change styling.: LLMs must simulate structural reasoning instead of acting on semantic cues — inflating prompt size and increasing hallucination risk.: Raw DOM provides no semantic understanding.: Every UI change requires agent updates

The disconnect between LLMs' natural language processing and websites' structural complexity creates agents that are fragile, expensive to maintain, and difficult to debug.

The Solution: Semantic Abstraction Through Perception

Notte introduces a that acts as a translation interface between websites and LLMs. Instead of forcing LLMs to parse DOM structures, it transforms raw web pages into structured, natural language descriptions that preserve semantic meaning while abstracting away implementation details.

The perception layer converts this:

<div class="product-card-container">
  <div class="product-image-wrapper">
    <img src="/product-123.jpg" alt="Wireless Headphones">
  </div>
  <div class="product-details">
    <h3 class="product-title">Premium Wireless Headphones</h3>
    <span class="price-current">$99.99</span>
    <button class="btn btn-primary add-to-cart" data-product-id="123">
      Add to Cart
    </button>
  </div>
</div>

Product: Premium Wireless Headphones
Price: $99.99
Image: Wireless Headphones
Available actions: Add to Cart

This transformation isn’t static — it’s context-aware and dynamic. The LLM now works with instead of .

Websites become navigable maps described in natural language. Instead of

driver.find_element(By.CSS_SELECTOR, ".add-to-cart")

your agent thinks: "Click the 'Add to Cart' button for Premium Wireless Headphones."

Natural language descriptions adapt better to UI changes than selectors. When developers change CSS classes from to , the perception layer still understands it as "Add to Cart button."

When semantic intent is preserved, perception remains robust — even when markup changes.

Information is presented in the format LLMs understand best — natural language with clear semantic structure. This improves reasoning, reduces hallucination risk, and shrinks prompt size.

4. Smaller Models, Better Performance

The perception layer enables smaller models (like the Llama suite) to reason effectively on simplified inputs. DOM noise is stripped away, letting inference engines focus on what matters. This allows smaller models to compete with larger ones in task-specific execution.

from notte_sdk import NotteClient

# Initialize Notte client
notte = NotteClient(api_key="your-api-key")

# Natural language task execution using the agents API
response = notte.agents.run(
    task="Find the cheapest wireless headphones under $100 and add them to cart"
)

print(response.answer)

Session-Controlled Advanced Example 

from notte_sdk import NotteClient
from pydantic import BaseModel

# Define the expected response schema
class TwitterPost(BaseModel):
    url: str

notte = NotteClient()

# Advanced session management with credentials
with notte.Vault() as vault, notte.Session(
    headless=False, 
    proxies=False, 
    browser_type="chrome"
) as session:

    # Secure credential management (use env vars in production)
    vault.add_credentials(
        url="https://x.com",
        username="your-email",      # Replace with your real email
        password="your-password"    # Replace with your real password
    )

    # Create agent with session context
    agent = notte.Agent(
        session=session,
        vault=vault,
        max_steps=10
    )

    # Complex multi-step workflow
    response = agent.run(
        task="go to twitter and post: new era this is @nottecore taking over my acc. Return the post url.",
        response_format=TwitterPost  # Triggers schema validation
    )

    print(f"Posted successfully: {response.answer.url}")

Data Extraction with Structured Output 

from notte_sdk import NotteClient
import json

notte = NotteClient()

# Structured data extraction using the scrape method
data = notte.scrape(
    url="https://pump.fun",
    instructions="get top 5 latest trendy coins on pf, return ticker, name, mcap"
)

# Print the result
print(json.dumps(data, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False))

Reduced Maintenance Overhead

When websites change their UI, natural language descriptions remain stable. Your agents continue working without constant selector updates.

Debug through natural language traces instead of cryptic DOM queries:

❌ Old way: "Element not found: button.submit-btn.primary"
✅ New way: "Could not find 'Submit Order' button on checkout page"

Faster Development Cycles

Write agent tasks in plain English instead of learning complex selector strategies:

# Instead of this:
element = driver.find_element(By.XPATH, 
    "//div[contains(@class, 'product-grid')]//div[contains(@class, 'product-item')][.//span[contains(text(), 'Wireless')]]//button[contains(@class, 'add-cart')]"
)

# Use this:
result = agent.run(task="Add wireless headphones to cart")

Better Multi-Step Workflows

Notte handles cookies, CAPTCHAs, and anti-bot protection while maintaining session state across complex workflows:

response = agent.run(task="""
1. Compare prices for iPhone 15 across 3 major retailers
2. Find the best deal including shipping costs
3. Check availability and delivery times
4. Generate a summary report with recommendations
""")

print(response.answer)

Notte outperforms all other web agents in speed, costs, and reliability by:

: Less verbose DOM parsing means fewer API calls: Semantic abstraction allows efficient models like Llama to excel: Ultra-high inference such as Cerebras without losing precision: Natural language understanding reduces task failures

(Explore our open operator evals here).

# Automated competitor price monitoring
response = agent.run(
    task="Check competitor pricing for wireless headphones on Amazon and Best Buy, compare with our $99 target price"
)

# Professional outreach automation
response = agent.run(
    task="Find 20 startup founders in the AI space on LinkedIn who recently posted about funding, extract their contact info"
)

# Automated market intelligence using the scrape endpoint
data = notte.scrape(
    url="https://www.g2.com/categories/project-management",
    instructions="Extract the top 5 project management tools with their pricing, ratings, and key features for competitive analysis"
)

Traditional web agents force LLMs to think like web scrapers.

Notte lets them think like decision-makers — understanding what to do, not just where to click.

This isn't just about making agents work better. It’s about making them maintainable, debuggable, and production-ready. When your agent understands “find the cheapest flight” instead of parsing div.flight-result-container > span.price-value, you've solved the fundamental problem of web automation.

Build web agents that understand meaning, not just markup.

Ready to build agents that actually understand websites?

items.title: Top 5 v0 Alternatives for AI-Powered UI Component Building
items.author: Harsh Singh
items.content:
The rise of AI-driven development tools has transformed how developers create user interfaces, making it faster and more accessible to build polished, production-ready UI components. Vercel's v0 has gained significant traction for its ability to generate React components from text prompts, leveraging AI to streamline front-end development. However, v0 is not the only player in this space. If you are exploring alternatives that offer unique features, better customization, or different workflows, this article highlights five compelling options, including Supafine, a promising AI-powered UI component builder for React. Let's dive into the top alternatives to v0 and what makes them stand out.

Supafine is an AI-powered UI component builder tailored for React developers, designed to generate clean, customizable, and production-ready components. Unlike some competitors, Supafine emphasizes seamless integration with modern React workflows, leveraging Tailwind CSS for styling. Its intuitive interface allows developers to describe UI requirements in natural language, and the AI generates React components that align with best practices, such as component-based architecture and accessibility.

  • : Generates components optimized for React ecosystems, including Next.js.
  • : Offers flexibility to tweak generated code, ensuring it fits your project's design system.
  • Integration with Modern Tools: Supports Tailwind CSS, Remix Icons, Recharts and other popular npm packages for rapid development.
  • : Reduces the need for manual code reviews by producing reliable, error-free components.

Supafine stands out for its focus on React developers who want AI-generated components that feel handcrafted. Its ability to understand complex requirements and produce components makes it a strong contender for teams building scalable applications. Give it a try at create.supafine.app and get 30 free credits every day.

Dyad is an open-source, local-first alternative to v0, designed for developers who prioritize control and privacy. It allows users to generate UI components and entire web applications using AI, with a focus on offline capabilities. Dyad supports multiple frameworks, including React, and is ideal for developers who want to experiment with AI-driven development without relying on cloud-based services.

  • : Free to use and customizable, with a transparent codebase.
  • : Runs locally, ensuring data privacy and no dependency on internet connectivity.
  • Compatibility: Supports React, Vue, and other frameworks.
  • : Benefits from contributions by developers worldwide.

Dyad is perfect for developers who value open-source principles and want a lightweight, customizable tool for generating UI components. Its offline capabilities make it unique, though it may lack the polish of cloud-based solutions like v0.

Bolt is another AI-driven tool that focuses on generating full-stack applications, including UI components, from high-level prompts. While it supports React, Bolt's strength lies in its ability to scaffold entire projects, making it a good fit for developers building MVPs or prototypes. It integrates with popular package managers like npm and supports modern frameworks like Next.js.

  • : Creates both front-end and back-end code, reducing setup time.
  • : Ideal for quickly iterating on app ideas.
  • : Allows developers to modify generated code to suit specific needs.
  • : Analyzes requirements to propose project structures.

Bolt is a great choice for developers who need more than just UI components and want a tool that can jumpstart an entire project. However, its broader scope may feel overwhelming for those focused solely on UI development.

Fine takes a unique approach by positioning its AI agents as "software developers that never sleep". It excels at understanding business requirements and generating production-ready React components, complete with testing and documentation. Fine's AI can analyze existing codebases to ensure consistency, making it a strong choice for enterprise teams or developers working on large-scale projects.

  • : Adapts to your project's coding style and standards.
  • : Generates, tests, and deploys components with minimal human intervention.
  • Technical Product Manager Mindset: Encourages clear, detailed prompts for better results.
  • : Handles tedious tasks like boilerplate code and testing.

Fine is ideal for teams that need AI to act as a virtual developer, handling repetitive tasks while maintaining high code quality. Its learning curve may be steeper due to the need for precise prompts, but the payoff is robust, production-ready components.

Same.new is an AI-powered platform focused on generating modern, responsive UI components for React and other frameworks. It emphasizes delivering clean, modular code that integrates seamlessly with existing projects. With a focus on developer experience, Same.New allows users to input design preferences or text prompts, producing components optimized for performance and scalability.

  • : Produces modular, well-documented React components.
  • : Works with React, Vue, and more.
  • : Ensures components are lightweight and efficient.
  • : Offers pre-built templates for common UI patterns.

Same.new is ideal for developers seeking a balance between rapid component generation and high-quality, production-ready code. Its focus on performance and modularity makes it a great fit for scalable applications, though it may lack some of the design-to-code features of other tools.

While v0 has set a high bar for AI-powered UI component generation, alternatives like Supafine, Dyad, Bolt, Fine, and Morph offer unique strengths that cater to different developer needs. Supafine shines for React developers seeking seamless integration and customization, while Dyad appeals to those who value open-source and offline capabilities. Bolt is perfect for full-stack prototyping. Depending on your project requirements, one of these tools could be the perfect fit to enhance your UI development workflow.

Explore these alternatives, starting with Supafine on Product Hunt, and find the tool that best aligns with your development goals. Happy coding!
